title: Test Pyramid — Many Unit, Some Integration, Few E2E
description:
Structure your test suite as a pyramid: ~70% unit tests (fast, isolated, cheap), ~20% integration tests (verify module interactions), ~10% end-to-end tests (critical user flows only). Unit tests catch logic errors fast; integration tests catch wiring bugs; e2e tests catch real-world failures. An inverted pyramid (mostly e2e) leads to slow, brittle, expensive suites.

Example ratio for a 500-test suite:
- 350 unit tests (run in <10s)
- 100 integration tests (run in <60s)
- 50 e2e tests (run in <5min)

title: Arrange-Act-Assert Structure for Every Test
Test Behavior, Not Implementation Details
No Test Interdependence — Each Test Runs Alone
description:
Every test must pass when run in isolation, in any order. Never rely on shared mutable state, test execution order, or side effects from other tests.

Anti-pattern:
```ts
let user;
test('creates user', () => { user = createUser(); });
test('updates user', () => { updateUser(user.id, { name: 'New' }); }); // fails if run alone
```

Fix: each test creates its own data in beforeEach or in the test body. Use fresh fixtures per test. Run tests with --randomize flag to catch hidden dependencies.

title: Mock at Boundaries, Not Internals
Use Test Factories for Complex Object Setup
One Logical Assertion Per Test
Flaky Tests — Never Use Sleep, Use Polling
Descriptive Test Names: should_expected_when_condition
description:
Test names should describe the expected behavior and the condition that triggers it. Use a consistent naming convention across the suite.

Good names:
```ts
test('should return 404 when user does not exist', ...)
test('should apply discount when cart total exceeds 100', ...)
test('should reject empty email on registration', ...)
```

Bad names:
```ts
test('test1', ...)
test('works correctly', ...)
test('handles edge case', ...)
```

When a test fails, its name should tell you what broke without reading the code.

title: Snapshot Testing Overuse
description:
Snapshot tests capture output and compare against a stored reference. They're useful for catching unexpected changes but dangerous when overused — developers blindly update snapshots without reviewing diffs.

Good use: serialized data structures, CLI output, small component renders.
Bad use: large component trees, frequently changing UI, API responses.

Rules:
- Keep snapshots small and focused
- Always review snapshot diffs in code review
- Use inline snapshots for small values: expect(result).toMatchInlineSnapshot()
- If a snapshot updates on every change, replace it with explicit assertions

title: Code Coverage Is a Guide, Not a Goal
description:
Aim for ~80% code coverage as a healthy baseline. Don't chase 100% — it leads to low-value tests that test getters, setters, and trivial code.

What to cover:
- Business logic and domain rules
- Error handling paths
- Edge cases and boundary conditions
- Integration points

What to skip:
- Simple getters/setters
- Framework boilerplate
- Generated code
- Type-only files

Coverage tells you what's NOT tested. High coverage doesn't mean good tests — you can have 100% coverage with zero meaningful assertions.

title: TDD Red-Green-Refactor Cycle
Testing Through the UI for Logic Validation
Parameterized Tests for Multiple Input Cases
